description: I observed the hummer at 3 different times (within the same 5 minute span). During the first two observations, the bird flew towards the feeder and then it flew away (so maybe 1-2 seconds each). The bird was very skittish and did not land. Right away, I noticed it was large with darker green coloration (when compared to the black-chinned and broad-tailed females that are also currently visiting the feeder). A few minutes later, the bird visited a third time. This time, I was waiting with my camera held to my face (ready and hoping it would appear). When it showed up, I took as many photos as possible. Based on the time stamps of the photos, I observed it for one minute. It has not shown up again. It appears to be a female Rivoli’s. The bird was significantly larger than the usual hummingbird species and I noticed the large size right away. The bird was (1) darkish green overall, (3) had a small post-ocular spot, (2) greenish spots on the throat and gray underparts, (3) long bill that had a very slight downward curve, and (4) small white tips on the tail (that is demonstrated in the photos). The female blue-throated mountain-gem would have more white in the tail, darker underparts, and a stronger post-ocular white stripe.
